food_subgroup: Vietnamese fresh (not fried) rice paper rolls with pork belly, shrimp, rice vermicelli, lettuce, mint, and perilla; served with hoisin-peanut dipping sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Goi Cuon (Vietnamese fresh rice paper roll). FOODON_03315674 (spring roll) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GMF is the closest FoodEx2 code available; no exact code exists for Goi Cuon specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Goi Cuon or Vietnamese fresh spring rolls.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving is 2 fresh rice paper rolls (~140g) plus approximately 2 tablespoons (40g) of hoisin-peanut dipping sauce, totalling approximately 180g and providing approximately 239 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Goi Cuon — FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia (SEA) 2020
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table 2020, which includes Goi Cuon as a composite dish entry. Values represent the combined rolls and dipping sauce as typically served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice paper and rice-based mixed dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Goi Cuon as a composite dish. GI ~55 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) rice paper (bánh tráng) GI ~61 when freshly rehydrated at room temperature (University of Sydney GI Database); (2) the moderating effect of peanut fat, protein from shrimp and pork, and dietary fibre from fresh vegetables and herbs, which collectively lower the effective GI of the composite dish below that of plain rice paper. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (55 × 30g) / 100 = 16.5. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~344mg per 100g; ~620mg per standard 180g serving (approximately 31% of the 2000mg daily limit), with hoisin-peanut dipping sauce contributing approximately 350mg of the total sodium.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume Goi Cuon in moderation and consider requesting reduced or no dipping sauce, as the sauce accounts for the majority of sodium; the rolls alone (~140g) contain approximately 270mg sodium, which is well within a single-meal sodium budget.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~55 (low), GL ~16.5 per standard serving; carbohydrate content ~30g per 180g serving, moderated by peanut fat, shrimp and pork protein, and vegetable fibre.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Goi Cuon is one of the lowest-GL Vietnamese restaurant options and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a starter; the peanut fat in the dipping sauce further slows gastric emptying and blunts the postprandial glucose response, though portion control of the hoisin sauce (which contributes ~3g sugar) is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.2g per 100g; ~2.2g per serving), zero trans fat, and the presence of anti-inflammatory polyphenols from fresh mint and perilla herbs.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Goi Cuon is a heart-friendly choice due to its low saturated fat content, absence of trans fat, and inclusion of fresh herbs with anti-inflammatory properties; sodium from the dipping sauce should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory fresh herbs (mint, perilla) and low saturated fat; shrimp provides zinc and selenium.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The fresh herb components (mint, perilla) provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols, and the absence of frying eliminates oxidised lipids, making Goi Cuon a suitable choice for individuals managing rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Goi Cuon (gỏi cuốn), known in English as Vietnamese fresh spring rolls or summer rolls, are a classic Vietnamese dish consisting of translucent rice paper wrappers (bánh tráng) filled with boiled pork belly, poached shrimp, cooked rice vermicelli, fresh lettuce, mint, and perilla (tía tô). Unlike fried spring rolls (chả giò / nem rán), Goi Cuon are assembled cold and served at room temperature, making them approximately 50% lower in calories and 70% lower in fat than their fried counterparts. The rolls are served with a hoisin-peanut dipping sauce that contributes the majority of the dish's sodium (~350mg of ~620mg total per serving). Per 100g: 133 kcal, 6.7g protein, 4.4g fat (1.2g saturated), 16.7g carbohydrate, 2.5g sugar, 1.0g dietary fibre, 344mg sodium. Per standard serving of 2 rolls + sauce (~180g): approximately 239 kcal, 12g protein, 8g fat, 30g carbohydrate, ~620mg sodium. GI ~55 (low), GL ~16.5 per serving — one of the lowest-GL Vietnamese restaurant items available. Clinically, Goi Cuon is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (low GI, moderate GL), hypertension (moderate sodium, primarily from sauce), and cardiovascular disease (low saturated fat, zero trans fat, anti-inflammatory herbs). Source: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table 2020.
common_user_queries: goi cuon vs fried spring rolls

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table 2020, an approved primary regional source for Southeast Asian dishes. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for rice paper, with documented moderating factors. All mandatory fields are complete and internally consistent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Goi Cuon as a composite dish is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA Table 2020. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA Table 2020. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per FAO/INFOODS SEA Table 2020 — no frying involved, no hydrogenated fats in ingredients). GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Goi Cuon as a composite dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The hoisin-peanut dipping sauce is included in the per-100g values as part of the composite dish entry. Halal status is not confirmed as pork belly is a primary ingredient — this dish is not suitable for halal dietary requirements. Two portions are listed in the input with identical gram weights (180g) but different descriptions; the canonical serving is standardised at 180g (140g rolls + 40g sauce).
